items.correct: A syntactic unit headed by a noun, which may include determiners, adjectives and modifiers — e.g. 'the big red car' is a noun phrase functioning as a single grammatical unit.
items.misconception: A noun phrase is just a noun by itself.
items.random_wrong: A noun phrase is a sentence that contains nouns.
items.why: Noun phrases can be one word ('cats') or many ('the cats that lived next door'). The phrase functions as a unit — subject, object or complement. Students reduce NP to a single noun, missing constituent structure.

items.q: What is Skinner's behaviourist view of language acquisition?
items.correct: Children learn language through imitation and operant conditioning — they are reinforced for correct utterances and corrected for errors.
items.misconception: Skinner believed in an innate grammar module.
items.random_wrong: Skinner believed children couldn't learn language until age five.
items.why: Skinner is the BEHAVIOURIST — the opposite of nativists like Chomsky. Students mix up the two. Skinner's view is environmental; Chomsky's is innate.

items.q: What is an adjacency pair in conversation analysis?
items.correct: A two-part sequence where the first part conditions the second — question/answer, greeting/greeting, request/acceptance-or-refusal. The second part is 'conditionally relevant' on the first.
items.misconception: Adjacency pairs are two people who sit next to each other while talking.
items.random_wrong: Adjacency pairs are pairs of synonyms in vocabulary lists.
items.why: Adjacency pairs reveal that turns aren't independent — a question expects an answer, and silence after one is heard as meaningful. Students take 'adjacency' too literally (physical proximity) rather than structural sequencing.

items.q: What does the L in PEEL stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Link. “List” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which reading skill hunts specific facts?
items.why: Correct answer: Scanning. “Skimming” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters in PEEL paragraph structure?
items.why: The real figure is 4 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
